expectations.text: Response uses content-appropriate stage names (not generic code stages)
expectations.evidence: Lines 18-55: brand/, research/, scripts/ (with brainstorming/, outlines/, drafts/, final/ substages), production/, planning/, reference/ — all content-appropriate names.

expectations.text: Response includes a dedicated voice or brand guidelines file with tone and audience sections
expectations.evidence: Lines 59-90: brand-voice-guide.md with Tone, Key Vocabulary, Audience Perspective, Signature Elements, What I Never Do sections.

expectations.text: Response uses structured stage contracts with Inputs/Process/Outputs format
expectations.evidence: No stage contracts. Lines 198-213 describe four independent session types ('Upload brand guide. Say: ...') — no Inputs tables, no numbered Process steps, no formal Outputs specifications.

expectations.text: Response explains how brand voice stays persistent across stages
expectations.evidence: Lines 198-199: 'Upload your brand-voice-guide.md at the start' for each session type. Line 264: 'The key is keeping your brand guide at the center.' Mechanism is manual upload rather than structural loading, but persistence is addressed.

expectations.text: Response explains how output from one stage feeds into the next
expectations.evidence: No pipeline flow described. Lines 198-213 show four independent session types (scripting, research, refinement, planning) without specifying how output from one feeds into the next as a sequential pipeline.

note: Iteration 2 baseline is now DISCRIMINATING (0.6 vs 1.0). The without-skill response knows content organization but lacks structured stage contracts and pipeline handoff — the two areas where ICM adds the most value.
